title: Diuretic Therapy
subtitle: The nurse manages diuretics — watching potassium (loop/thiazide lower it, potassium-sparing raise it), weighing the patient daily, and timing doses in the morning.

patient.name: Diuretics
patient.admitDate: The nurse safely administers and monitors diuretic therapy.

nursesNote: The nurse safely administers diuretics, which remove excess fluid and sodium and are used for heart failure, edema, and hypertension. The main classes differ in how they affect potassium. Loop diuretics such as furosemide are strong and fast-acting and waste potassium; thiazide diuretics such as hydrochlorothiazide are milder and also lower potassium; potassium-sparing diuretics such as spironolactone retain potassium and can cause it to rise. The nurse monitors the potassium level closely (low with loop and thiazide, high with potassium-sparing) and watches for signs of low potassium such as muscle weakness, cramps, and an irregular heartbeat. Patients on loop or thiazide diuretics are encouraged to eat potassium-rich foods or take supplements as ordered, while patients on potassium-sparing diuretics avoid potassium supplements and salt substitutes. The nurse weighs the patient daily at the same time, on the same scale, in similar clothing, since weight is the best measure of fluid change, and tracks intake and output and blood pressure while watching for dehydration. Doses are given in the morning to avoid waking at night to void. The nurse teaches the patient to rise slowly to prevent orthostatic dizziness and to follow the diet appropriate to their diuretic type.
providerOrders: Monitor the serum potassium and intake and output
Weigh the patient daily at the same time and scale
Give diuretic doses in the morning
Assess for signs of low potassium and dehydration
Teach diet and safety based on the diuretic type

opts.text: They remove excess fluid and sodium
Loop diuretics like furosemide lower potassium
Spironolactone is potassium-sparing
All diuretics raise the potassium level
Daily weight is the best measure of fluid change
Diuretics are best given at bedtime
Weakness and cramps can signal low potassium
Potassium-sparing diuretics can cause high potassium
Salt substitutes are safe with spironolactone
Loop/thiazide patients may need potassium-rich foods
Rising slowly helps prevent orthostatic dizziness
Intake and output should be tracked

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order giving a morning dose of furosemide

opts.text: Check the latest potassium level
Assess blood pressure and hydration
Give the dose in the morning
Monitor intake, output, and daily weight
Teach potassium-rich foods and rising slowly
Furosemide (loop)
Spironolactone (potassium-sparing)
Hydrochlorothiazide (thiazide)
Adding a salt substitute with spironolactone
Eating bananas while on furosemide
Heavy diuresis with a loop diuretic

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient on furosemide has weakness, cramps, and K 2.9
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recognize hypokalemia and check the patient
Place on a cardiac monitor
Notify the provider of the low potassium
Give potassium replacement as ordered
Recheck potassium and document
Weighing daily at the same time and scale
Taking furosemide right before bed
Eating bananas and oranges while on a loop diuretic
Using a potassium salt substitute with spironolactone
Rising slowly from lying to standing
Ignoring muscle weakness and an irregular pulse
Monitoring potassium on any diuretic
Stopping intake and output once edema improves
